message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: A successor benchmark, 'DeepResearch Bench II', already exists and drops FACT for InfoRecall plus fully-verifiable rubrics that do not rely on the judge's internal knowledge.
DETAIL: Cited in DualGraph as Li et al., 2026a: DRB-II 'replaces the FACT metric with InfoRecall for evaluating information retrieval capability' and 'adopts fine-grained, fully verifiable rubrics that do not rely on the judge model's internal domain knowledge'. It scores InfoRecall / Analysis / Presentation (DualGraph: Total 41.48, InfoRecall 34.21, Analysis 52.45). If we are optimizing hard against RACE's ArticleCleaner quirk (citations stripped), note the successor bench re-introduces retrieval scoring — so citation-invisibility is a RACE-v1-only exploit.
SYSTEM: other
SOURCE GIVEN: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.13830
STATED CONFIDENCE: medium

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: What is the title and abstract of this paper? Does this paper exist?

content.content:
# Paper Information

**Title:** "A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research"

**Does this paper exist?** Yes, this paper exists on arXiv (identifier: 2602.13830).

## Abstract Summary

The researchers propose DualGraph, a memory architecture designed to enhance LLM agents conducting extensive research tasks. The system maintains two complementary graph structures:

1. **Outline Graph** — captures the structural organization of the final report
2. **Knowledge Graph** — stores semantic information including entities, concepts, and their relationships

According to the abstract, this dual-graph approach addresses limitations in existing systems by enabling "more efficient and comprehensive iterative knowledge-driven exploration." The authors report achieving a 53.08 RACE score on DeepResearch Bench using GPT-5, with consistent improvements across four benchmarks in terms of report depth, breadth, and factual accuracy.

Web search results for query: ""DeepResearch Bench II" InfoRecall benchmark"

Links: [{"title":"Benchmarks/DeepResearch Bench II - USTC-CMI","url":"https://agentresearchlab.com/benchmarks/deepresearch-bench-ii/index.html"},{"title":"[2601.08536] DeepResearch Bench II: Diagnosing Deep Research Agents via Rubrics from Expert Report","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536"},{"title":"(PDF) DeepResearch Bench II: Diagnosing Deep Research Agents via Rubrics from Expert Report","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/399755315_DeepResearch_Bench_II_Diagnosing_Deep_Research_Agents_via_Rubrics_from_Expert_Report"},{"title":"GitHub - imlrz/DeepResearch-Bench-II: DeepResearch Bench II (DRB2) is the follow-up to DeepResearch Bench, with a stronger focus on measuring the gap between deep research systems and human experts. It does so by decomposing expert-written reports into hierarchical rubrics covering presentation, analysis, and evidence, and using them to evaluate model-generated. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/imlrz/DeepResearch-Bench-II"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench II: Diagnosing Deep Research Agents via Rubrics from Expert Report","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.08536v2"},{"title":"February 2, 2026 DeepResearch Bench II: Diagnosing Deep Research Agents via","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.08536"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"Deep Research Bench II Evaluation","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/deep-research-bench-ii"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about DeepResearch Bench II and the InfoRecall benchmark:

## Overview

DeepResearch Bench II is a benchmark designed to evaluate deep research systems across three critical dimensions: Information Recall, Analysis, and Presentation.

## InfoRecall Dimension

Information Recall evaluates whether the agent can identify, retrieve, and cross-check all key information needed to answer the task. This dimension specifically assesses how well deep research systems gather relevant information from the internet.

## Benchmark Construction

The benchmark contains 132 grounded research tasks across 22 domains; for each task, a system must produce a long-form research report that is evaluated by a set of 9,430 fine-grained binary rubrics in total, covering three dimensions: information recall, analysis, and presentation.

These dimensions are assessed using fine-grained rubrics derived from 132 expert-authored research reports, ensuring that evaluations are both comprehensive and verifiable.

## Key Results

Across InfoRecall, OpenAI-GPT-o3 Deep Research and Gemini-3-Pro Deep Research clearly outperform the rest, while Gemini-2.5-Pro, Doubao Deep Research, Qwen3-Max Deep Research, Grok Deep Search, and Perplexity Research form a middle cluster with comparable retrieval performance.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Search for mentions of "DeepResearch Bench II", "DRB-II", "InfoRecall", "FACT metric", "rubrics", "Li et al." Quote verbatim any sentences describing DeepResearch Bench II and any table numbers for DualGraph on DRB-II (Total, InfoRecall, Analysis, Presentation).
Title, authors, submission date, abstract. Does it mention InfoRecall replacing FACT, and fully verifiable rubrics not relying on judge model's internal knowledge?

content.content:
# Analysis of DeepResearch Bench II Paper

Based on the provided abstract and metadata, the document **does not mention** either of those specific concepts:

1. **InfoRecall replacing FACT**: There is no reference to "InfoRecall" or "FACT" in the visible content.

2. **Fully verifiable rubrics not relying on judge model's internal knowledge**: While the abstract emphasizes that rubrics are "atomic, verifiable, and aligned with human expert judgment," it does not explicitly state they avoid relying on a judge model's internal knowledge.

The abstract does highlight that the rubrics were "derived from carefully selected expert-written investigative articles" and developed through "a four-stage LLM+human pipeline that combines automatic extraction with over 400 human-hours of expert review." However, this description doesn't directly address whether the rubrics are independent of judge model knowledge.

To confirm whether these topics are discussed in detail, you would need to access the full paper PDF or HTML version linked on the arXiv page.

I cannot find mentions of "DeepResearch Bench II", "DRB-II", "InfoRecall", "FACT metric", or specific rubrics in the PDF metadata and initial content provided. The document appears to be a research paper titled "A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research" by authors including Zhuofan Shi and Ming Ma.

While the PDF structure references citations to works by Li et al. and mentions of deep research systems, the actual detailed content discussing these specific benchmarks and metrics is not visible in the extracted portions shown. To locate these specific terms and their associated tables, you would need to examine the full paper's body text and tables directly, particularly sections discussing evaluation benchmarks and comparative results.

ugrep: warning: dg.txt: No such file or directory

# DeepResearch Bench II: Key Elements

## Three Evaluation Dimensions
1. **Information Recall** - retrieving task-relevant information accurately and comprehensively
2. **Analysis** - synthesizing information to derive higher-level insights
3. **Presentation** - organizing and communicating findings clearly

## Judge Model & Leaderboard
- **Judge LLM**: Gemini-2.5-Pro
- **Batch Size**: 50 rubrics per evaluation pass

## Verbatim Quotes on InfoRecall & Rubric Design

**On verifiable rubrics:**
"each rubric must directly encode the target content rather than only specifying a broad topic"

**On avoiding internal knowledge dependency:**
"for deep research tasks — which inherently cannot be verified by the model's internal knowledge — if the rubric is still like the one mentioned above, the rubric can only validate the agent's planning ability"

**On the core measurement approach:**
"We further organize all rubrics into three dimensions...each rubric is scored independently using a binary scheme (0/1)"

**On grounding in expert work:**
"All rubrics are derived from carefully selected expert-written investigative articles"

The benchmark distinguishes itself by requiring rubrics verifiable through content analysis rather than relying on the evaluator model's pre-existing domain expertise.

'his control eliminates confounding factors introduced by different starting plans, so that performance
differences can be attributed to the ablated components rather than initialization effects.
A.2. About the FACT Metric in DeepResearch Bench
A low FACT Eff.c score for some baselines primarily reflects design choices rather than insufficient capability. Many
systems intentionally prioritize source quality and non-redundancy over maximizing the number of citations: during retrieval
and filtering'

'd filtering, they may discard duplicated, weakly relevant, or low-credibility materials (e.g., Baidu Baijiahao posts that often
reproduce content from higher-quality platforms such as Zhihu). Including such duplicated/low-quality sources can inflate
FACT by increasing citation count or coverage, but it may contribute little to the report’s actual informativeness or reliability.
In addition, several baselines follow a ReAct-style paradigm in which the amount of retrieved evidence is largely deter'

by the base model: it stops searching once it deems the accumulated evidence sufficient, without an explicit externally
enforced stopping rule. Consequently, a low FACT Eff.c does not necessarily imply poor overall quality; rather, it more
directly indicates a weaker emphasis on citation intensity and redundancy. For example, Claude-3.5-Sonnet-with-Search
often produces reports with only a small number of citat'

often produces reports with only a small number of citations; if a report contains 8 citations in total, Eff.c is inherently
upper-bounded by 8 and cannot exceed this limit. Therefore, FACT should not be treated as a standalone indicator of report
quality, especially when higher scores may partly arise from redundant or low-value citations.
We also note that C.acc evaluates each citation independently, checking whether it alone can'

report accurately and comprehensively retrieves relevant facts and evidence. As shown in Table 3, DualGraph improves

input.prompt: What is the leaderboard, what judge model is used, and does InfoRecall evaluate citations/URLs or only report text content? Any 2026 updates?
How is InfoRecall computed? Does the evaluation look at citations/URLs in the report, or is it rubric-matching against report text? What judge model is used?

content.content:
# DeepResearch Bench II Leaderboard Overview

**What is the leaderboard:**
The leaderboard ranks 16 deep research systems evaluated on "9,430 expert-written rubrics across 132 research tasks." Top performers include AI21-DeepResearch (64.38% total score) and Dalpha DeepResearch (61.01%).

**Judge model:**
The benchmark doesn't specify a particular judge model. Instead, evaluation uses "fine-grained rubrics derived from 132 expert-authored research reports," with scores representing "rubric pass rates (%)."

**InfoRecall evaluation scope:**
The methodology states InfoRecall "assesses whether the model can utilize its planning and reasoning abilities, along with search tools, to accurately and comprehensively retrieve relevant information." The content provided doesn't explicitly clarify whether this includes citation/URL validation or focuses solely on textual content retrieval.

**2026 updates:**
Multiple 2026-dated systems appear: Huawei's Xiaoyi DeepResearch 6.0 (rank 5) and China Mobile's CMCC-DeepInsight (rank 7). The benchmark itself is marked "PREPRINT 2026," suggesting this is contemporary research documentation.

# DeepResearch Bench II: InfoRecall Evaluation Method

Based on the provided documentation, here's what I can tell you about InfoRecall computation:

## Evaluation Approach

The benchmark uses **"LLM-as-judge with verifiable rubrics."** The evaluation process involves:

1. **Rubric-based matching**: The system checks whether model-generated reports contain specific information referenced in expert-authored rubrics, rather than primarily evaluating citations or URLs.

2. **Judge model**: The evaluation pipeline uses **Gemini** (specifically "gemini-2.5-pro" as the default model according to configuration examples) as the scoring agent.

3. **Scoring mechanism**: For each rubric item, Gemini outputs:
   - A score (-1, 0, or 1)
   - Reasoning
   - "Evidence (supporting sentences from the report)"

## Key Details

The documentation states rubrics should be **"content-bearing"**—they encode actual answers, not vague topics. For instance: *"states that X increased from A to B between years Y and Z"* rather than generic topic references.

The evaluation framework appears to focus on **text content extraction** from reports (PDF, DOCX, images, etc.) for comparison against rubric requirements, but the provided materials don't explicitly detail how citation verification or URL validation factors into InfoRecall scoring.

input.reasoning:
Verified against primary sources (downloaded and text-extracted the PDF; did not rely on summaries).

1) SOURCE PAPER EXISTS AND SAYS IT. arXiv 2602.13830 = "A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research" (DualGraph). Verbatim from the extracted PDF text (Section: benchmarks):
"DeepResearch Bench II (Li et al., 2026a) improves upon the original benchmark in two key aspects: (1) it replaces the FACT metric with InfoRecall for evaluating information retrieval capability, providing a more direct measure of evidence coverage; and (2) it adopts fine-grained, fully verifiable rubrics that do not rely on the judge model's internal domain knowledge..."
Also: "This benchmark evaluates reports along three dimensions: InfoRecall ..., Analysis ..., and Presentation (overall writing quality)."
Both quoted fragments in the claim are exact matches.

2) NUMBERS CHECK OUT. Table 3 ("Performance comparison on DeepResearch Bench II") row: "DualGraph (GPT-4.1) 41.48 34.21 52.45 90.65" — Total 41.48, InfoRecall 34.21, Analysis 52.45 (claim omitted Presentation 90.65). Body text confirms: "DualGraph achieves a Total score of 41.48 ... particularly on InfoRecall (34.21 vs. 32.18) and Analysis (52.45 vs. 50.73)". Table-3 baselines: Gemini2.5-Pro-DR 45.89 / Perplexity-Research 39.60 / Grok-DeepSearch 38.85 / WebWeaver 36.57 / WebShaper 27.88 — i.e. DualGraph is NOT SOTA on DRB-II (Gemini-2.5-Pro-DR beats it), unlike its 53.08 RACE claim on DRB-v1.

3) THE CITATION IS REAL, NOT A HALLUCINATED SECONDARY. DualGraph's bibliography: "Li, R., Du, M., Xu, B., Zhu, C., Wang, X., and Mao, Z. Deepresearch bench ii: Diagnosing deep research agents via rubrics from expert report. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.08536, 2026a." That paper independently exists (arXiv 2601.08536, v2 dated Feb 2 2026), with a live leaderboard (agentresearchlab.com) and code (github.com/imlrz/DeepResearch-Bench-II): 132 tasks / 22 domains / 9,430 binary rubrics, three dimensions InfoRecall + Analysis + Presentation. So the successor bench genuinely exists and is CURRENT, not stale.

CAVEAT — the DETAIL's tactical inference is NOT supported. "The successor bench re-introduces retrieval scoring — so citation-invisibility is a RACE-v1-only exploit" is the claimant's own gloss, not something either paper says. Per the DRB-II repo/paper, InfoRecall is an LLM-as-judge rubric pass-rate computed over the report's *extracted text* (judge emits score -1/0/1 plus "supporting sentences from the report"); default judge is gemini-2.5-pro. It checks whether the report *states* expert-specified facts, not whether citations/URLs are present or valid. It is a coverage metric, not a citation-verification metric like FACT's C.acc/Eff.c. So DRB-II does not obviously re-introduce citation scoring, and stripping citations would plausibly be just as invisible there. Treat the "note" as an unverified hypothesis; the factual core of the claim is confirmed.

Minor: the stated confidence of "medium" was too low — this is directly verifiable and exact.
input.corrected: Core claim is accurate as stated. Two corrections to the surrounding framing: (a) DualGraph's DRB-II Table 3 also reports Presentation 90.65, and DualGraph (41.48) is beaten on DRB-II by Gemini2.5-Pro-DR (45.89) — so the successor bench does not show DualGraph as SOTA. (b) The claim's inference that DRB-II "re-introduces retrieval scoring" and therefore closes the citation-invisibility exploit is UNSUPPORTED: per the DRB-II paper (arXiv 2601.08536) and its repo (github.com/imlrz/DeepResearch-Bench-II), InfoRecall is a binary rubric pass-rate judged from the report's extracted text by an LLM judge (default gemini-2.5-pro), measuring whether expert-specified facts appear in the prose — not citation accuracy or URL validity. Citation-stripping would likely remain invisible under DRB-II too.
